title: Electoral Pluralism Index
subtitle: Data by the [Economist Intelligence Unit](#dod:economist-intelligence-unit). Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.
caption:
Electoral Pluralism Index

Data by the Economist Intelligence Unit. Expert estimates of the extent to which all citizens can choose their political leaders in free and fair elections. Higher values indicate more pluralism.

The choropleth map displays the Electoral Pluralism Index for 2024, using a continuous color scale from 1 to 10 where deep red represents the lowest scores and dark blue represents the highest scores. The highest values, falling in the 8 to 10 range, appear across western and central Europe, including France, Germany, and the Nordic countries, as well as in Canada, the United States, Australia, and New Zealand. In South America, Argentina and Brazil sit in the 6 to 8 band, while Venezuela falls in the 1 to 2 range. Across sub-Saharan Africa, the map shows a concentration of low scores, with countries like Chad, Sudan, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo in the 1 to 2 band, and Nigeria and Ethiopia in the 2 to 4 band. In Asia, India and Indonesia fall in the 4 to 6 range, while China and Russia are in the 1 to 2 band. The Middle East shows predominantly low scores, with Saudi Arabia, Iran, and Yemen in the 1 to 2 range. Greenland and several small island nations are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data. The color scale progresses through red (1-3), orange (3-5), yellow (5-6), light blue (6-8), and dark blue (8-10). The most visible pattern is the geographic clustering of high scores in western Europe, North America, and Oceania, contrasted with low scores across much of Africa, the Middle East, and parts of Asia.
